questions.question: Kinetic theory explains the behavior
questions.question_raw: Kinetic theory explains the behavior

options.label: A
options.text: of solids and liquids based on the idea that they gas consist of rapidly vibrating atoms or molecules

options.label: B
options.text: of solids based on the idea that the solid consists of rapidly vibrating atoms or molecules

options.label: C
options.text: of gases based on the idea that the gas consists of rapidly moving atoms or molecules

options.label: D
options.text: of liquids based on the idea that the liquids consist of rapidly moving atoms or molecules

correct_option_texts: of gases based on the idea that the gas consists of rapidly moving atoms or molecules
questions.explanation:
The theory for ideal gases makes the following assumptions
1. Gases consist of particles in constant, random motion. They continue in a straight line until they collide with something—usually each other or the walls of their container.
2. Particles are point masses with no volume. The particles are so small compared to the space between them, that we do not consider their size in ideal gases.
3. No molecular forces are at work. This means that there is no attraction or repulsion between the particles.
4. Gas pressure is due to the molecules colliding with the walls of the container. All of these collisions are perfectly elastic, meaning that there is no change in energy of either the particles or the wall upon collision. No energy is lost or gained from collisions.
5. The time it takes to collide is negligible compared with the time between collisions.
6. The kinetic energy of a gas is a measure of its Kelvin temperature. Individual gas molecules have different speeds, but the temperature and kinetic energy of the gas refer to the average of these speeds.
7. The average kinetic energy of a gas particle is directly proportional to the temperature. An increase in temperature increases the speed in which the gas molecules move.
8. All gases at a given temperature have the same average kinetic energy.
9. Lighter gas molecules move faster than heavier molecules.

questions.question: Kinetic theory:
questions.question_raw: Kinetic theory:

options.label: A
options.text: correctly explains the specific heat capacities of many liquids

options.label: B
options.text: correctly explains the specific heat capacities of many gases

options.label: C
options.text: correctly explains specific heat capacities of many solids

options.label: D
options.text: correctly explains specific heat capacities of super cooled liquids

correct_option_texts: correctly explains the specific heat capacities of many gases
questions.explanation: The kinetic theory describes a gas as a large number of submicroscopic particles (atoms or molecules), all of which are in constant rapid motion that has randomness arising from their many collisions with each other and with the walls of the container. It will apply only to gases.

questions.question: Kinetic theory relates measurable properties.
questions.question_raw: Kinetic theory relates measurable properties.

options.label: A
options.text: of super cooled liquids such as viscosity, creep and diffusion with molecular parameters, yielding estimates of molecular sizes and masses

options.label: B
options.text: of gases such as viscosity, conduction and diffusion with molecular parameters, yielding estimates of molecular sizes and masses

options.label: C
options.text: of solids such as expansion, conduction and elongation with molecular parameters, yielding estimates of molecular sizes and masses

options.label: D
options.text: of liquids such as viscosity, conduction and diffusion with molecular parameters, yielding estimates of molecular sizes and masses

correct_option_texts: of gases such as viscosity, conduction and diffusion with molecular parameters, yielding estimates of molecular sizes and masses
questions.explanation: Kinetic theory explains the behaviour of gases based on the idea that the gas consists of rapidly moving atoms or molecules. It also relates measurable properties of gases such as viscosity, conduction and diffusion with molecular parameters, yielding estimates of molecular sizes and masses.

questions.question: According to Atomic Hypothesis:
questions.question_raw: According to Atomic Hypothesis:

options.label: A
options.text: All things are made of atoms - little particles that move around in circles

options.label: B
options.text: All things are made of atoms - little particles that stay put at fixed points

options.label: C
options.text: All things are made of atoms - little particles that move around in perpetual motion

options.label: D
options.text: All things are made of atoms - little particles that keep oscillating

correct_option_texts: All things are made of atoms - little particles that move around in perpetual motion
questions.explanation: The atomic hypothesis that all things are made of atoms-little particles that move around in perpetual motion, attracting each other when they are a little distance apart, but repelling upon being squeezed into one another.

questions.question: According to Avogadro’s law:
questions.question_raw: According to Avogadro’s law:

options.label: A
options.text: Equal volumes of all gases at equal temperature and pressure have different numbers of molecules.

options.label: B
options.text: Equal volumes of all gases at equal temperatures have the same number of molecules.

options.label: C
options.text: Equal volumes of all gases at equal pressure have the same number of molecules.

options.label: D
options.text: Equal volumes of all gases at equal temperature and pressure have the same number of molecules.

correct_option_texts: Equal volumes of all gases at equal temperature and pressure have the same number of molecules.
questions.explanation:
Avogadro's law states that "equal volumes of all gases, at the same temperature and pressure, have the same number of molecules"
For a given mass of an ideal gas, the volume and amount (moles) of the gas are directly proportional if the temperature and pressure are constant.
V $ \propto $ n
${V \over n} = k$

questions.question: In which case are the atoms relatively rigidly fixed?
questions.question_raw: In which case are the atoms relatively rigidly fixed?
questions.explanation:
Atoms in a gas are well separated with no regular arrangement. Atoms vibrate and move freely at high speeds
Atoms in a liquid are close together with no regular arrangement. Atoms vibrate, move about, and slide past each other.
Atoms in a solid are tightly packed, usually in a regular pattern. Atoms vibrate (jiggle) but generally do not move from place to place.

questions.question: The order of interatomic distance in liquids is about:
questions.question_raw: The order of interatomic distance in liquids is about:
questions.explanation: Using the assumption of little spheres (for interatomic space) the spacing between the molecules is about 2 $\times$ 10^-10 m i.e.$2\mathop A\limits^o $

questions.question: The average distance a molecule can travel without colliding is called the:
questions.question_raw: The average distance a molecule can travel without colliding is called the:
questions.explanation: The mean free path is the average distance travelled by a moving particle (such as an atom, a molecule, a photon) between successive impacts (collisions), which modify its direction or energy or other particle properties.

questions.question: In dynamic equilibrium, molecules collide and change their speeds during the collision:
questions.question_raw: In dynamic equilibrium, molecules collide and change their speeds during the collision:

options.label: C
options.text: but the average properties are constant

correct_option_texts: but the average properties are constant
questions.explanation: All collisions between molecules among themselves or between molecules and the walls are elastic. So that total kinetic energy and total momentum are conserved. Also, the average properties of gas remain constant.

questions.question: A cubic vessel (with faces horizontal + vertical) contains an ideal gas at NTP. The vessel is being carried by a rocket which is moving at a speed of 500ms^-1 in vertical direction. The pressure of the gas inside the vessel as observed by us on the ground
questions.question_raw: A cubic vessel (with faces horizontal + vertical) contains an ideal gas at NTP. The vessel is being carried by a rocket which is moving at a speed of 500ms-1 in vertical direction. The pressure of the gas inside the vessel as observed by us on the ground

options.label: A
options.text: remains the same because the motion of the vessel as a whole does not affect the relative motion of the gas molecules and the walls.

options.label: B
options.text: will be different on the top wall and bottom wall of the vessel.

options.label: C
options.text: will increase by a factor equal to $\frac{\left(V_{\mathrm{rms}}^2+(500)^2\right)}{v^{22}}$ where v_rms was the original mean square velocity of the gas.

options.label: D
options.text: remain the same because 500 ms^-1 is very much smaller than v_rms of the gas.

correct_option_texts: remains the same because the motion of the vessel as a whole does not affect the relative motion of the gas molecules and the walls.
questions.explanation:
According to the ideal gas law,
P = $\frac{nRT}{V}$, here the temperature of the vessel remains unchanged hence, the pressure remains the same from that point of view.
Now, let us discuss the phenomenon inside the vessel. The gas molecules keep on colliding among themselves as well as with the walls of containing vessel. These collisions are perfectly elastic.
The number of collisions per unit volume in a gas remains constant. So, the pressure of the gas inside the vessel remains the same because the motion of the vessel as a whole does not affect the relative motion of the gas molecules with respect to the walls.

questions.question: Consider a mixture of non-interacting ideal gases: ${\mu _{\rm{1}}}$ moles of gas 1, ${\mu _{\rm{2}}}$ moles of gas 2, etc. in a vessel of volume V at temperature T and pressure P. It is then found that the equation of state of the mixture is :
questions.question_raw: Consider a mixture of non-interacting ideal gases: {tex}{\mu _{\rm{1}}}{/tex} moles of gas 1, {tex}{\mu _{\rm{2}}}{/tex} moles of gas 2, etc. in a vessel of volume V at temperature T and pressure P. It is then found that the equation of state of the mixture is :
questions.explanation:
Total pressure = Sum of partial pressure 
= P_1 + P_2 +.........
P = $ {{{\mu _1}RT} \over V} + {{{\mu _2}RT} \over V} $ +........ 
PV = ($\mu_ {\rm{1}}$ + ${\mu _{\rm{2}}}$ +…) RT

questions.question: According to Dalton’s law of partial pressures:
questions.question_raw: According to Dalton’s law of partial pressures:

options.label: A
options.text: the total pressure of a mixture of real gases is the sum of factored individual pressures

options.label: B
options.text: the total pressure of a mixture of ideal gases is the difference of partial pressures

options.label: C
options.text: the total pressure of a mixture of ideal gases is the sum of pressures

options.label: D
options.text: the total pressure of a mixture of ideal gases is the sum of partial pressures

correct_option_texts: the total pressure of a mixture of ideal gases is the sum of partial pressures
questions.explanation:
According to Dalton's law of partial pressures, at constant volume and temperature, the total pressure exerted by a mixture of gases is equal to the sum of the partial pressures of the component gases
P = P_1 + P_2 + .......
P = $\frac{\mu_{1} R T}{V}$ + $\frac{\mu_{2} R T}{V}$ + ........
PV = $(\mu_{1}+\mu_{2}+.....)$ RT
PV = $\mu $RT

questions.question: Considering a gas in a parallelopiped, in a small time interval $\Delta {\rm{t}}$, a molecule with x-component of velocity v_x will hit the wall?
questions.question_raw: Considering a gas in a parallelopiped, in a small time interval {tex}\Delta {\rm{t}}{/tex}, a molecule with x-component of velocity vx will hit the wall?

options.label: A
options.text: If it is outside or beyond the distance v_x $\Delta {\rm{t}}$ from the wall

options.label: B
options.text: If it is outside or beyond the distance 2v_x $\Delta {\rm{t}}$ from the wall

options.label: C
options.text: If it is within the distance v_x $\Delta {\rm{t}}$ from the wall

options.label: D
options.text: If it is within the distance 2v_x $\Delta {\rm{t}}$ from the wall

correct_option_texts: If it is within the distance v_x $\Delta {\rm{t}}$ from the wall
questions.explanation:
Collision by molecule to the wall of container assumed to be perfectly elastic.
By the principle of conservation of momentum, the momentum imparted to the wall in the collision = 2mv_x
So the magnitude of the force on the wall from each collision is
$F = {{2m{v_x}} \over {\Delta t}}$ 
distance travelled in time $\Delta t$ 
$x = {v_x}\Delta t$

questions.question: If there are n number of molecules per unit volume and m is the mass of each, v_x is the x-component of velocity, pressure can be written as:
questions.question_raw: If there are n number of molecules per unit volume and m is the mass of each, vx is the x-component of velocity, pressure can be written as:
questions.explanation:
Pressure is due to collision of molecule with the wall of container.
Collision by molecule to the wall of container assumed to be perfectly elastic.
By the principle of conservation of momentum, the momentum imparted to the wall in the collision = 2mv_x 
the number of molecules hitting the wall in time $\Delta t$ = $nA{v_x}\Delta t$ 
n = no. of molecule per unit volume
A = Area of container
But, on the average, half of these are moving towards the wall and the other half away from the wall. Thus, the number of molecules with velocity (v_x , v_y, v_z) hitting the wall in time ∆t is ½A v_x∆t n, where n is the number of molecules per unit volume. The total momentum transferred to the wall by these molecules in time ∆t is: Q = (2mv_x ) (½ n A v_x ∆t )
The total momentum transferred to the wall by these molecules in $\Delta t$ time = $m{v_x}\left( {nA{v_x}\Delta t} \right)$ 
Pressure P = $F\over A$ = rate of momentum transfer per unit area
P = $ {{m{v_x}\left( {nA{v_x}\Delta t} \right)/\Delta t} \over A} $ = $nm{v^2}_x$ 
all molecules in a gas do not have the same velocity; there is a distribution in velocities. The above equation therefore, stands for pressure due to the group of molecules with speed v_x in the x-direction and n stands for the number density of that group of molecules. The total pressure due to all groups 
P = $ nm\overline {v_x^2} $

questions.question: The average kinetic energy of a molecule:
questions.question_raw: The average kinetic energy of a molecule:

options.label: A
options.text: is inversely proportional to the molecular mass of the gas

options.label: B
options.text: is inversely proportional to the absolute temperature of the gas

options.label: C
options.text: is proportional to the absolute temperature of the gas

options.label: D
options.text: is not dependent on the absolute temperature of the gas

correct_option_texts: is proportional to the absolute temperature of the gas
questions.explanation:
$E = {3 \over 2}kT$ 
E $\propto$ T

questions.question: According to the law of equipartition of energy, the total energy is equally distributed in all possible energy modes, with each mode having average energy equal to:
questions.question_raw: According to the law of equipartition of energy, the total energy is equally distributed in all possible energy modes, with each mode having average energy equal to:
questions.explanation: The law of equipartition of energy states that for any dynamical system in thermal equilibrium, the total energy is equally divided among the degrees of freedom. The energy associated with a molecule for one degree of freedom is $\frac{1}{2}{\text{ }}{{\text{k}}_{\text{B}}}{\text{T}}$

questions.question: The number of degrees of freedom a diatomic molecule is:
questions.question_raw: The number of degrees of freedom a diatomic molecule is:
questions.explanation:
Degrees of freedom of a system refers to the possible independent motions a system can have.
the total degrees of freedom describing the motion of a diatomic molecule is 5.
3 for translation and 2 for rotation

questions.question: The number of degrees of freedom a monoatomic molecule have:
questions.question_raw: The number of degrees of freedom a monoatomic molecule have:
questions.explanation:
Degrees of freedom of a system refers to the possible independent motions a system can have.
Monoatomic gas molecule can have 3 independent motion and hence have 3 degrees of freedom (all are translational).

questions.question: The molar specific heat at constant volume, C_v for monatomic gases is:
questions.question_raw: The molar specific heat at constant volume, Cv for monatomic gases is:
questions.explanation:
C_V = $\frac{1}{2}Rf$ 
for monatomic gases f = 3
C_V = $\frac{3}{2}R$

questions.question: The molar specific heat at constant volume, C_v for diatomic gases is:
questions.question_raw: The molar specific heat at constant volume, Cv for diatomic gases is:
questions.explanation:
C_V =$ \frac{1}{2}$ Rf 
for diatomic gases f = 5
C_V = $\frac{5}{2}R$

questions.question: For a rope of yield strength S_y loaded in tension with weight Mg the minimum area A of the rope should be:
questions.question_raw: For a rope of yield strength Sy loaded in tension with weight Mg the minimum area A of the rope should be:
questions.explanation:
We know that Yield strength times the Area gives the weight of the body.
As Yield strength = $\frac{\text { weight of body }}{\text { area }}$
For safety purposes
Yield strength $ \geq $ $\frac{\text { weight of body }}{\text { area }}$
${{\rm{S}}_{\rm{y}}}$ $ \geq $ $\frac{Mg}{A}$
A $ \geq $ $\frac{Mg}{{\rm{S}}_{\rm{y}}}$

questions.question: Mean free path is the:
questions.question_raw: Mean free path is the:
questions.explanation: the mean free path is the average distance traveled by a moving particle (such as an atom, a molecule, a photon) between successive impacts (collisions), which modify its direction or energy or other particle properties.

questions.question: An approximate equation for calculating the mean free path is:
questions.question_raw: An approximate equation for calculating the mean free path is:
questions.explanation:
If the molecules have diameter d, then the effective cross-section for a collision can be A = $ \pi {d^2}$ 
l = distance travelled
n = no. of molecule per unit volume
V = volume of interaction
The mean free path could be taken as the length of the path divided by the number of collisions.
mean free path
$\lambda=\frac{l}{V \times n}=\frac{l}{(A l) \times n}=\frac{\bar{v} t}{\left(\pi d^2 \times \bar{v} t\right) \times n}$
$\lambda=\frac{1}{\pi d^2 n}$

questions.question: Estimate the fraction of molecular volume to the actual volume occupied by oxygen gas at STP. Take the diameter of an oxygen molecule to be 3 $\mathop A\limits^0 $.
questions.question_raw: Estimate the fraction of molecular volume to the actual volume occupied by oxygen gas at STP. Take the diameter of an oxygen molecule to be 3 {tex}\mathop A\limits^0 {/tex}.
questions.explanation:
Radius = $1.5 \mathop A\limits^o $ 
molecular volume of oxygen 
$V = {N_A} \times {4 \over 3}\pi {r^3} $ = 6.023 $\times$ 10^23 $\times$ $4\over 3$ $\times$ 3.14 $\times$(1.5 $\times$10^−8)^3 = 8.51cm^3
Actual volume of oxygen at STP = 22400cm^3
ratio of molecular volume to the actual volume is given by = ${{8.51} \over {22400}} = 3.8 \times {10^{ - 4}}$

questions.question: Molar volume is the volume occupied by 1 mol of any (ideal) gas at standard temperature and pressure (STP : 1 atmospheric pressure, 0 ^oC). The value of Molar volume is:
questions.question_raw: Molar volume is the volume occupied by 1 mol of any (ideal) gas at standard temperature and pressure (STP : 1 atmospheric pressure, 0 oC). The value of Molar volume is:

questions.question: The figure shows a plot of $\frac{PV}{T}$versus P for 1.00 $ \times $ 10^−3 kg of oxygen gas at two different temperatures. The dotted plot corresponds to
questions.question_raw: The figure shows a plot of {tex}\frac{PV}{T}{/tex}versus P for 1.00 {tex} \times {/tex} 10−3 kg of oxygen gas at two different temperatures. The dotted plot corresponds to
questions.explanation:
For 1 mole of ideal gas, according to ideal gas equation​​​​
PV = RT
$\frac{PV}{T}$ = RT = const.
Hence the graph must be with zero slopes. So that dotted line show ‘ideal’ gas behaviour and the curved line shows deviation from ‘ideal’ gas behaviour.

questions.question: The figure shows a plot of $\frac{PV}{T}$ versus P for 1.00$ \times $ 10^−3 kg of oxygen gas at two different temperatures. Comparing T_1 and T_2.
questions.question_raw: The figure shows a plot of {tex}\frac{PV}{T}{/tex} versus P for 1.00{tex} \times {/tex} 10−3 kg of oxygen gas at two different temperatures. Comparing T1 and T2.
questions.explanation:
For 1 mole of an ideal gas, according to the ideal gas equation
​​​​​PV = RT
$\frac{PV}{T}$ = R = constant
Hence the graph must be with zero slopes. So that dotted line show ‘ideal’ gas behaviour and the curved line show deviation from ‘ideal’ gas behaviour.
A real gas behaves as an ideal gas at high temperature. Temperature T_1 is close to the dotted line. 
so that T_1 > T_2

questions.question: The figure shows a plot of $\frac{PV}{T}$ versus P for 1.00$ \times $ 10^−3 kg of oxygen gas at two different temperatures. What is the value of$\frac{PV}{T}$ where the curves meet on the y-axis?
questions.question_raw: The figure shows a plot of {tex}\frac{PV}{T}{/tex} versus P for 1.00{tex} \times {/tex} 10−3 kg of oxygen gas at two different temperatures. What is the value of{tex}\frac{PV}{T}{/tex} where the curves meet on the y-axis?
questions.explanation:
PV = nRT
$\frac{PV}{T}$ = nR = $\frac{1}{32}$ $\times$ 8.314 = 0.26 JK^-1
Hence the value of $\frac{PV}{T}$ where the curves meet on the y-axis is 0.26 J K^−1

questions.question: An oxygen cylinder of volume 30 liters has an initial gauge pressure of 15 atm and a temperature of 27 ^oC. After some oxygen is withdrawn from the cylinder, the gauge pressure drops to 11 atm and its temperature drops to 17 ^oC. Estimate the mass of oxygen taken out of the cylinder (R = 8.31 J mol^-1 K^-1, the molecular mass of O_2 = 32 u).
questions.question_raw: An oxygen cylinder of volume 30 liters has an initial gauge pressure of 15 atm and a temperature of 27 oC. After some oxygen is withdrawn from the cylinder, the gauge pressure drops to 11 atm and its temperature drops to 17 oC. Estimate the mass of oxygen taken out of the cylinder (R = 8.31 J mol-1 K-1, the molecular mass of O2 = 32 u).
questions.explanation:
V_1 = 30lit = 30 $\times$ 10^−3m^3
P_1 = 15atm = 15 $\times$ 1.01 $\times$ 10^5 = 15.15 $\times$ 10^5Pa
T_1 = 27^oC = 300K
P_1V_1 = n_1RT_1
$n_{1}=\frac{P_{1} V_{1}}{R T_{1}}=\frac{15.15 \times 10^{5} \times 30 \times 10^{-3}}{8.31 \times 300}$ = 18.276
$n_{1}=\frac{m_{1}}{M}$
m_1 = n_1M = 18.276 $\times$ 32 = 584.84gm
When some oxygen is withdrawn from the cylinder 
V_2 = 30lit = 30 $\times$ 10^−3m^3
P_1 = 11atm = 11 $\times$ 1.01 $\times$ 10^5 = 11.11 $\times$ 10^5Pa
T_1 = 17^oC = 300K
P_2V_2 = n_2RT_2
$n_{2}=\frac{P_{2} V_{2}}{R T_{2}}=\frac{11.11 \times 10^{5} \times 30 \times 10^{-3}}{8.31 \times 290}$ = 13.86
${n_2} = {{{m_2}} \over M}$ = 
m_2 = n_2M = 13.86 $\times$ 32 = 453.1gm
the mass of oxygen taken out of the cylinder = m_1 − m_2 = 584.84 − 453.1 = 131.74gm = 0.13Kg.

questions.question: An air bubble of volume 1.0 cm^3 rises from the bottom of a lake 40 m deep at a temperature of 12 ^oC. To what volume does it grow when it reaches the surface, which is at a temperature of 35 ^oC?
questions.question_raw: An air bubble of volume 1.0 cm3 rises from the bottom of a lake 40 m deep at a temperature of 12 oC. To what volume does it grow when it reaches the surface, which is at a temperature of 35 oC?
questions.explanation:
P_1 = P_2 +h$\rho$g
V_1 = 1.0cm^3
h = 40m
T_1 = 35^oC = 308K , density of water is = 10^3 kg m^-3
Pressure at surface P_2 = 1atm = 1.013$\times$ 10^5Pa
Pressure at depth of 40 meter = P_1 = 1atm + h$\rho$g = (1.013 $\times$ 10^5) + 40 $\times$ 10^3 $\times$ 9.8 = 493300Pa
${{{P_1}{V_1}} \over {{T_1}}} = {{{P_2}{V_2}} \over {{T_2}}} $
${V_2} = {{{P_1}{V_1}{T_2}} \over {{P_2}{T_1}}} $ = $ {{493300 \times 1.0 \times {{10}^{ - 6}} \times 308} \over {1.013 \times {{10}^5} \times 285}} $ = 5.3 $\times$ 10^−6m^3.

questions.question: Estimate the total number of air molecules (inclusive of oxygen, nitrogen, water vapor and other constituents) in a room of capacity 25.0 m^3 at a temperature of 27 ^oC and 1 atm pressure.
questions.question_raw: Estimate the total number of air molecules (inclusive of oxygen, nitrogen, water vapor and other constituents) in a room of capacity 25.0 m3 at a temperature of 27 oC and 1 atm pressure.
questions.explanation:
PV = NkT
N = $\frac{P V}{k T}$ = $\frac{1.01 \times 10^{5} \times 25}{1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 300}$ = 6.1 $\times$ 10^26

questions.question: Estimate the average thermal energy of a helium atom at room temperature (27 ^oC)
questions.question_raw: Estimate the average thermal energy of a helium atom at room temperature (27 oC)
questions.explanation:
E = $ {3 \over 2}kT $ = $3\over 2$ $\times$ 1.38 $\times$10^−23 $\times$ 300 = 6.2 $\times$ 10^−21J
k = Boltzmann constant

questions.question: Estimate the average thermal energy of a helium atom at the temperature on the surface of the Sun (6000 K)
questions.question_raw: Estimate the average thermal energy of a helium atom at the temperature on the surface of the Sun (6000 K)
questions.explanation:
E = $ {3 \over 2}kT $ = 32 $\times$ 1.38 $\times$ 10^−23 $\times$ 6000 = 1.24 $\times$ 10^−19J
k = boltzman constant

questions.question: Estimate the average thermal energy of a helium atom the temperature of 10 million Kelvin (the typical core temperature in the case of a star).
questions.question_raw: Estimate the average thermal energy of a helium atom the temperature of 10 million Kelvin (the typical core temperature in the case of a star).
questions.explanation:
E = $3\over2$kT = $3\over2$ $\times$ 1.38 $\times$ 10^−23 $\times$ 10 $\times$ 10^6 = 2.07 $\times$ 10^−16J 
k = Boltzmann constant

questions.question: Three vessels of equal capacity have gases at the same temperature and pressure. The first vessel contains neon (monatomic), the second contains chlorine (diatomic), and the third contains uranium hexafluoride (polyatomic). The number of molecules:
questions.question_raw: Three vessels of equal capacity have gases at the same temperature and pressure. The first vessel contains neon (monatomic), the second contains chlorine (diatomic), and the third contains uranium hexafluoride (polyatomic). The number of molecules:

options.label: A
options.text: is the greatest in the vessel with neon

options.label: B
options.text: is the greatest in the vessel with chlorine

options.label: C
options.text: is the same in all three vessels

options.label: D
options.text: is the greatest in the vessel with uranium hexafluoride

correct_option_texts: is the same in all three vessels
questions.explanation: Avogadro's law states that "equal volumes of all gases, at the same temperature and pressure, have the same number of molecules".

questions.question: Three vessels of equal capacity have gases at the same temperature and pressure. The first vessel contains neon (monatomic), the second contains chlorine (diatomic), and the third contains uranium hexafluoride (polyatomic). In which case is v_rms the largest?
questions.question_raw: Three vessels of equal capacity have gases at the same temperature and pressure. The first vessel contains neon (monatomic), the second contains chlorine (diatomic), and the third contains uranium hexafluoride (polyatomic). In which case is vrms the largest?

options.label: A
options.text: v_rms is the same in all vessels.

options.label: C
options.text: v_rms is largest for uranium hexafluoride.

options.label: D
options.text: v_rms is the largest for neon.

correct_option_texts: v_rms is the largest for neon.
questions.explanation:
${v_{rms}} = \sqrt {{{3RT} \over M}}$
${v_{rms}} \propto {1 \over {\sqrt M }}$ 
Neon has smaller molecular wt in comparison to chlorine and uranium hexafluoride. So that v_rms s largest for neon.

questions.question: At what temperature is the root mean square speed of an atom in an argon gas cylinder equal to the rms speed of a helium gas atom at – 20 ^oC? (Atomic mass of Ar = 39.9 u, of He = 4.0 u).
questions.question_raw: At what temperature is the root mean square speed of an atom in an argon gas cylinder equal to the rms speed of a helium gas atom at – 20 oC? (Atomic mass of Ar = 39.9 u, of He = 4.0 u).
questions.explanation:
v_Ar = v_He
$\sqrt{\frac{3 R T}{39.9}}=\sqrt{\frac{3 R \times 253}{4}}$
T = $ {{39.9 \times 253} \over 4} $ = 2.52 $\times$ 10^3K

questions.question: Estimate the mean free path and collision frequency of a nitrogen molecule in a cylinder containing nitrogen at 2.0 atm and temperature 17^oC. Take the radius of a nitrogen molecule to be roughly 1.0 $\mathop A\limits^0 $.
questions.question_raw: Estimate the mean free path and collision frequency of a nitrogen molecule in a cylinder containing nitrogen at 2.0 atm and temperature 17oC. Take the radius of a nitrogen molecule to be roughly 1.0 {tex}\mathop A\limits^0 {/tex}.
questions.explanation:
$\lambda = {1 \over {\sqrt 2 \pi {d^2}{n_V}}}$ 
n_V = no.of molecule per unit volume ($N\over V$)
PV = NkT
${n_V} = {N \over V} = {P \over {kT}}$
$\lambda = {{kT} \over {\sqrt 2 \pi {d^2}P}} $ = $ {{1.38 \times {{10}^{ - 23}} \times 290} \over {1.41 \times 3.14 \times {{\left( {2 \times {{10}^{ - 10}}} \right)}^2} \times \left( {2 \times 1.01 \times {{10}^5}} \right)}}$
$\lambda = 1.11 \times {10^{ - 7}}m$ 
collision frequency${{{v_{rms}}} \over \lambda }$ 
${v_{rms}} = \sqrt {{{3RT} \over M}} = \sqrt {{{3 \times 8.31 \times 290} \over {28 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}}} $ = 508.26m/sec 
collision frequency $ = {{508.26} \over {1.11 \times {{10}^{ - 7}}}} $ = 4.58 $\times$ 10^9s^−1

questions.question: In constructing a large mobile, an artist hangs an aluminum sphere of mass 6.0 kg from a vertical steel wire 0.50 m long and 2.5 $ \times $ 10^−3 cm^2 in a cross-sectional area. On the bottom of the sphere, he attaches a similar steel wire, from which he hangs a brass cube of mass 10.0 kg. Compute the tensile strain?
questions.question_raw: In constructing a large mobile, an artist hangs an aluminum sphere of mass 6.0 kg from a vertical steel wire 0.50 m long and 2.5 {tex} \times {/tex} 10−3 cm2 in a cross-sectional area. On the bottom of the sphere, he attaches a similar steel wire, from which he hangs a brass cube of mass 10.0 kg. Compute the tensile strain?
questions.explanation:
Given mass of aluminium sphere m_sph = 6.0 KG
length of steel wire L_steel = 0.50 m area of steel wire A_steel = 2.5 $\times$ 10^-3 cm^2
mass of cube m_cube = 10 KG
net restoring force in lower wire i.e. tension T_2 will be equal to weight
of cube i.e. T_2 = m_cube = 10g
net restoring force in upper wire i.e. tension T_1
T_1 = m_sph + T_2 = 6g + 10g = 16g
standard value for young modulus of steel y = 2 $\times$ 10^11 N/m^2
y = $\frac{\text { tensile stress }}{\text { tensile strain }}$ for lower wire
$\operatorname{strain}_{l w}=\frac{\text {tensile stress}}{y}=\frac{10 \times 9.8}{2.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 10^{-4} \times 2.0 \times 10^{11}}$
$\text {strain}_{l w} = 1.96 \times 10^{-3} \approx 2.0 \times 10^{-3} $ $\rightarrow$(1)
now for upper wire
$\operatorname{strain}_{u p} = \frac{\text {tensile stress}}{y} = \frac{16 \times 9.8}{2.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 10^{-4} \times 2.0 \times 10^{11}}$
strain_up = 3.14 $\times$ 10^−3 $\Rightarrow$ (2)

questions.question: A tank used for filling helium balloons has a volume of 0.300 m^3 and contains 2.00 mol of helium gas at 20.0 ^oC. Assuming that the helium behaves like an ideal gas, What is the average kinetic energy per molecule?
questions.question_raw: A tank used for filling helium balloons has a volume of 0.300 m3 and contains 2.00 mol of helium gas at 20.0 oC. Assuming that the helium behaves like an ideal gas, What is the average kinetic energy per molecule?
questions.explanation: E = $3\over 2$kT = $3\over 2$ $\times$ 1.38 $\times$ 10^−23 $\times$ 293 = 6.07 $\times$ 10^−21J

questions.question: Air at 20.0 ^oC in the cylinder of a diesel engine is compressed from an initial pressure of 1.00 atm and a volume of 800.0 cm^3 to a volume of 60.0 cm^3. Assume that air behaves as an ideal gas with $\gamma $ = 1.4 and that the compression is adiabatic. Find the final pressure of the air.
questions.question_raw: Air at 20.0 oC in the cylinder of a diesel engine is compressed from an initial pressure of 1.00 atm and a volume of 800.0 cm3 to a volume of 60.0 cm3. Assume that air behaves as an ideal gas with {tex}\gamma {/tex} = 1.4 and that the compression is adiabatic. Find the final pressure of the air.
questions.explanation:
$P_{1} V_{1}^{\gamma}=P_{2} V_{2}^{\gamma}$
P_2 = $P_{1}\left(\frac{V_{1}}{V_{2}}\right)^{\gamma}=1 \times\left(\frac{800}{60}\right)^{1.4}$ = 37.6atm

questions.question: Air at 20.0$^\circ {\rm{C}}$ in the cylinder of a diesel engine is compressed from an initial pressure of 1.00 atm and a volume of 800.0 cm^3 to a volume of 60.0 cm^3. Assume that air behaves as an ideal gas with $\gamma = 1.4$ and that the compression is adiabatic. Find the final temperature of the air.
questions.question_raw: Air at 20.0{tex}^\circ {\rm{C}}{/tex} in the cylinder of a diesel engine is compressed from an initial pressure of 1.00 atm and a volume of 800.0 cm3 to a volume of 60.0 cm3. Assume that air behaves as an ideal gas with {tex}\gamma = 1.4{/tex} and that the compression is adiabatic. Find the final temperature of the air.

questions.question: Nine particles have speeds of 5.00, 8.00, 12.0, 12.0, 12.0, 14.0, 14.0, 17.0, and 20.0 m/s. find the particles’ average speed.
questions.question_raw: Nine particles have speeds of 5.00, 8.00, 12.0, 12.0, 12.0, 14.0, 14.0, 17.0, and 20.0 m/s. find the particles’ average speed.

questions.question: Nine particles have speeds of 5.00, 8.00, 12.0, 12.0, 12.0, 14.0, 14.0, 17.0, and 20.0 m/s. What is the rms speed?
questions.question_raw: Nine particles have speeds of 5.00, 8.00, 12.0, 12.0, 12.0, 14.0, 14.0, 17.0, and 20.0 m/s. What is the rms speed?

questions.question: Approximate the air around you as a collection of nitrogen molecules, each of which has a diameter of 2.00 $ \times $ 10^-10 m. How far does a typical molecule move before it collides with another molecule?
questions.question_raw: Approximate the air around you as a collection of nitrogen molecules, each of which has a diameter of 2.00 {tex} \times {/tex} 10-10 m. How far does a typical molecule move before it collides with another molecule?
questions.explanation:
no. of molecule per unit volume 
n_V = ${N \over V} = {P \over {kT}} = {{1.01 \times {{10}^5}} \over {1.38 \times {{10}^{ - 23}} \times 293}} $ = 2.5 $\times $ 10^25
mean free path 
$\lambda = {1 \over {\sqrt 2 \pi {d^2}{n_V}}} = {1 \over {1.41 \times 3.14 \times {{\left( {2 \times {{10}^{ - 10}}} \right)}^2} \times 2.5 \times {{10}^{25}}}} $ = 2.25 $\times $ 10^−7m

questions.question: Approximate the air around you as a collection of nitrogen molecules, each of which has a diameter of 2.00 $ \times $ 10^−10 m. On average, how frequently does one molecule collide with another at 298K?
questions.question_raw: Approximate the air around you as a collection of nitrogen molecules, each of which has a diameter of 2.00 {tex} \times {/tex} 10−10 m. On average, how frequently does one molecule collide with another at 298K?
questions.explanation:
collision frequency $ = {1 \over \tau }$ = $ {{{v_{rms}}} \over \lambda }$ 
no. of molecule per unit volume 
n_V = $ {N \over V} = {P \over {kT}} = {{1.013 \times {{10}^5}} \over {1.38 \times {{10}^{ - 23}} \times 293}} $ = 2.5 $\times$ 10^25
mean free path 
$\lambda $ = $ {1 \over {\sqrt 2 \pi {d^2}{n_V}}} = {1 \over {1.41 \times 3.14 \times {{\left( {2 \times {{10}^{ - 10}}} \right)}^2} \times 2.5 \times {{10}^{25}}}} $ = 2.25 $\times$ 10^−7m
${v_{rms}} = \sqrt {{{3RT} \over M}} = \sqrt {{{3 \times 8.31 \times 298} \over {28 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}}} $= 515m/sec
 hence, collision frequency is given by $ = {1 \over \tau } = {{{v_{rms}}} \over \lambda } = {{515} \over {2.25 \times {{10}^{ - 7}}}} $ = 2.28 $\times$ 10^9/sec

questions.question: In a 30.0-s interval, 500 hailstones strike a glass window with an area of 0.600 m^2 at an angle of 45.0^o to the window surface. Each hailstone has a mass of 5.00 g and a speed of 8.00 m/s. If the collisions are elastic, what are the average force and pressure on the window?
questions.question_raw: In a 30.0-s interval, 500 hailstones strike a glass window with an area of 0.600 m2 at an angle of 45.0o to the window surface. Each hailstone has a mass of 5.00 g and a speed of 8.00 m/s. If the collisions are elastic, what are the average force and pressure on the window?
questions.explanation:
$F = 500 \times {{\Delta p} \over {\Delta t}} $
$= 500 \times {{2mv\cos 45^\circ } \over {\Delta t}} $
$= {{500 \times 2 \times 5 \times {{10}^{ - 3}} \times 8 \times 1} \over {30 \times 1.41}} $
F = 0.943N

questions.question: In a 30.0-s interval, 500 hailstones strike a glass window with an area of 0.600 m^2 at an angle of 45.0^o to the window surface. Each hailstone has a mass of 5.00 g and a speed of 8.00 m/s. If the collisions are elastic, what is the pressure on the window?
questions.question_raw: In a 30.0-s interval, 500 hailstones strike a glass window with an area of 0.600 m2 at an angle of 45.0o to the window surface. Each hailstone has a mass of 5.00 g and a speed of 8.00 m/s. If the collisions are elastic, what is the pressure on the window?
questions.explanation:
$ P = {F \over A}$
$F = 500 \times {{\Delta p} \over {\Delta t}} $
$= 500 \times {{2mv\cos 45^\circ } \over {\Delta t}} $
$= {{500 \times 2 \times 5 \times {{10}^{ - 3}} \times 8 \times 1} \over {30 \times 1.41}}$
A = 0.6 m^2
P = $ {{500 \times 2 \times 5 \times {{10}^{ - 3}} \times 8 \times 1} \over {30 \times 1.41 \times 0.6}} $ = 1.57Pa

questions.question: A 14.5 kg mass, fastened to the end of a steel wire of unstretched length 1.0 m, is whirled in a vertical circle with an angular velocity of 2 rev/s at the bottom of the circle. The cross-sectional area of the wire is 0.065 cm^2. Calculate the elongation of the wire when the mass is at the lowest point of its path.
questions.question_raw: A 14.5 kg mass, fastened to the end of a steel wire of unstretched length 1.0 m, is whirled in a vertical circle with an angular velocity of 2 rev/s at the bottom of the circle. The cross-sectional area of the wire is 0.065 cm2. Calculate the elongation of the wire when the mass is at the lowest point of its path.
questions.explanation:
given mass m = 14.5 kg
length of steel wire L = 1.0m
angular velocity $\omega$ = 2rev/s $\Rightarrow$ 2 $\times$ 2$\pi$rad/s = 4$\pi$rad/s cross sectional area of steel wire = 0.065cm^2 = 0.065 $\times$10^−4m^2
let us consider $\Delta$L be the elongation in steel wire when mass is at lowest point during its motion in vertical circle and T be the tension in wire apply in g_net force = centripetal force
T − mg = m$\omega$^2r
T = (14.5 $\times$ 9.8) + 14.5 $\times$ (4$\pi$)^2 $\times$ 1
T = 2429.5N now young modulus of steel wire y = 2.0 $\times$ 10^11pa
also $\mathrm{y}=\frac{\mathrm{stress}}{\operatorname{strain}}=\frac{\mathrm{T} \times \mathrm{L}}{\mathrm{A} \times \Delta \mathrm{L}}$
$\Delta \mathrm{L}=\frac{\mathrm{T} \times \mathrm{L}}{\mathrm{A} \times \mathrm{y}}=\frac{2429.5 \times 1.0}{0.065 \times 10^{-4} \times 2.0 \times 10^{11}}$
$\Delta$L = 1.9 $\times$ 10^−3m = 1.9mm

questions.question: A spherical balloon with a volume of 4 .0 x 10^3 cm^3 contains helium at an (inside) pressure of 1.20 $ \times $ 10^5 Pa. How many moles of helium are in the balloon if each helium atom has an average kinetic energy of 3.60 $ \times $ 10^-22 J?
questions.question_raw: A spherical balloon with a volume of 4 .0 x 103 cm3 contains helium at an (inside) pressure of 1.20 {tex} \times {/tex} 105 Pa. How many moles of helium are in the balloon if each helium atom has an average kinetic energy of 3.60 {tex} \times {/tex} 10-22 J?
questions.explanation:
We know that ,$P = {2 \over 3}{N \over V}\left( {\bar K} \right)$
$N = {3 \over 2}{{PV} \over {\left( {\bar K} \right)}} = {3 \over 2} \times {{\left( {1.20 \times {{10}^5}} \right)\left( {4.00 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}} \right)} \over {3.60 \times {{10}^{ - 22}}}} $ = 2 $\times$ 10^24
n = $ {N \over {{N_A}}} = {{2 \times {{10}^{24}}} \over {6.023 \times {{10}^{23}}}} $ = 3.32mole

questions.question: How many atoms of helium gas fill a balloon of diameter 30.0 cm at 20.0^oC and 1.00 atm?
questions.question_raw: How many atoms of helium gas fill a balloon of diameter 30.0 cm at 20.0oC and 1.00 atm?
questions.explanation:
PV = NkT
N = $\frac{P V}{k T}$ = $\frac{1.01 \times 10^{5} \times\left(\frac{4}{3} \times 3.14 \times(0.15)^{3}\right)}{1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 293}$ = 3.53 $\times$ 10^23

questions.question: What is the average kinetic energy of the helium atoms in a balloon of diameter 30.0 cm at 20.0^oC and 1.00 atm?
questions.question_raw: What is the average kinetic energy of the helium atoms in a balloon of diameter 30.0 cm at 20.0oC and 1.00 atm?
questions.explanation: $\bar K = {3 \over 2}kT = {3 \over 2} \times 1.38 \times {10^{ - 23}} \times 293 $ = 6.07 $\times$ 10^−21J

questions.question: What is the root-mean-square speed of helium atoms in a balloon of diameter 30.0 cm at 20.0^oC and 1.00 atm?
questions.question_raw: What is the root-mean-square speed of helium atoms in a balloon of diameter 30.0 cm at 20.0oC and 1.00 atm?
questions.explanation: v_rms = $ \sqrt {{{3RT} \over M}} = \sqrt {{{3 \times 8.31 \times 293} \over {4 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}}} $ = 1351m/sec = 1.35Km/sec

questions.question: A cylinder contains a mixture of helium and argon gas in equilibrium at 150^oC. What is the average kinetic energy for each type of gas molecule?
questions.question_raw: A cylinder contains a mixture of helium and argon gas in equilibrium at 150oC. What is the average kinetic energy for each type of gas molecule?
questions.explanation:
Average KE depends only on temperature
$\bar K $ = $3\over 2$k_BT = $3\over 2$ $\times$ 1.38 $\times$ 10^−23 $\times$ 423 = 8.76 $\times$ 10^−21J

questions.question: Anvils made of single crystals of diamond, with the shape as shown in Figure, are used to investigate the behaviour of materials under very high pressures. Flat faces at the narrow end of the anvil have a diameter of 0.50 mm, and the wide ends are subjected to a compression force of 50,000 N. What is the pressure at the tip of the anvil?
questions.question_raw: Anvils made of single crystals of diamond, with the shape as shown in Figure, are used to investigate the behaviour of materials under very high pressures. Flat faces at the narrow end of the anvil have a diameter of 0.50 mm, and the wide ends are subjected to a compression force of 50,000 N. What is the pressure at the tip of the anvil?
questions.explanation:
Given compression force F = 50000 N
Radius will be r = $\frac{diameter}{2}$ = $\frac{0.50}{2}$ = 0.25 mm = 0.25 $\times$ 10^-3 m
Area at which compression is applied A = $\pi$r^2 = 3.14 $\times$ (0.25 $\times$ 10^-3)^2 = 0.2 $\times$ 10^-6 m^2 
Pressure at the tip P = $\frac{Force}{area}$ = $\frac{50000}{0.2 \times 10^{-6}}$
P = 2.5 $\times$ 10^11 pa

questions.question: Calculate the change in internal energy of 3.00 mol of helium gas when its temperature is increased by 2.00 K.
questions.question_raw: Calculate the change in internal energy of 3.00 mol of helium gas when its temperature is increased by 2.00 K.
questions.explanation:
Helium is a monoatomic gas.(C_V = 1.5R)
change in internal energy 
$\Delta U $ = $ n{C_V}\Delta T $ 
= 3 $\times$ 1.5 $\times$ 8.31 $\times$ 2 = 75J

questions.question: One mole of hydrogen gas is heated at constant pressure from 300 K to 420 K. Calculate the energy transferred by heat to the gas
questions.question_raw: One mole of hydrogen gas is heated at constant pressure from 300 K to 420 K. Calculate the energy transferred by heat to the gas
questions.explanation:
Hydrogen is a diatomic gas. (C_P = 3.5R)
energy transferred by heat to the gas
Q = $ n{C_P}\Delta T $ = 1 $\times$ 3.5 $\times$ 8.31 $\times$ 120 = 3490J = 3.49KJ

questions.question: One mole of hydrogen gas is heated at constant pressure from 300 K to 420 K. Calculate the increase in its internal energy.
questions.question_raw: One mole of hydrogen gas is heated at constant pressure from 300 K to 420 K. Calculate the increase in its internal energy.
questions.explanation:
Hydrogen is a diatomic gas. (C_V = 2.5R)
Change in internal energy 
$\Delta U = n{C_V}\Delta T $ = 1 $\times$ 2.5 $\times$ 8.31 $\times$ 120 = 2493J = 2.49KJ

questions.question: One mole of hydrogen gas is heated at constant pressure from 300 K to 420 K. Calculate the work done by the gas.
questions.question_raw: One mole of hydrogen gas is heated at constant pressure from 300 K to 420 K. Calculate the work done by the gas.
questions.explanation:
An isobaric process 
W = nR(T_F − T_I) = 1 $\times$ 8.31 $\times$ (420 − 300) = 0.98kJ

questions.question: A house has well-insulated walls. It contains a volume of 100 m^3 of air at 300 K. Calculate the energy required to increase the temperature of this diatomic ideal gas by 1.00 ^oC. $\left(C_P=\frac{7}{2} R\right)$
questions.question_raw: A house has well-insulated walls. It contains a volume of 100 m3 of air at 300 K. Calculate the energy required to increase the temperature of this diatomic ideal gas  by 1.00 oC. {tex}\left(C_P=\frac{7}{2} R\right){/tex}
questions.explanation:
Q = $ n{C_P}\Delta T $ = $\frac 7 2 Rn\Delta T $ = $\frac{7}{2}\left(\frac{P V}{T}\right) \Delta T$ (because, PV=nRT thus n = $\frac {PV}{ RT}$)
Q = $\frac{7}{2} \times\left(\frac{1.01 \times 10^5 \times 100}{300}\right) \times 1$ = 118KJ

questions.question: A house has well-insulated walls. It contains a volume of 100 ${{\rm{m}}^{\rm{3}}}$ of air at 300 K. If the energy required to lift an object of mass m through a height of 2.00 m equals the energy required to increase the temperature of this air by 1.00^oC., what is the value of m? $\left(C_P=\frac{7}{2} R\right)$
questions.question_raw: A house has well-insulated walls. It contains a volume of 100 {tex}{{\rm{m}}^{\rm{3}}}{/tex} of air at 300 K. If the energy required to lift an object of mass m through a height of 2.00 m equals the energy required to increase the temperature of this air by 1.00oC., what is the value of m? {tex}\left(C_P=\frac{7}{2} R\right){/tex}
questions.explanation:
Q = $n C_{P} \Delta T=\frac{7}{2} R n \Delta T=\frac{7}{2}\left(\frac{P V}{T}\right) \Delta T$
Q = $\frac{7}{2} \times\left(\frac{1.01 \times 10^{5} \times 100}{300}\right) \times 1$ = 118 K J
U = mgy
m = $\frac{U}{g y}=\frac{118 \times 10^{3}}{9.8 \times 2}$ = 6.03 $\times$ 10^3Kg

questions.question: One mole of an ideal monatomic gas is at an initial temperature of 300 K. The gas undergoes an isovolumetric process, acquiring 500 J of energy by heat. It then undergoes an isobaric process, losing this same amount of energy by heat. Determine the new temperature of the gas:
questions.question_raw: One mole of an ideal monatomic gas is at an initial temperature of 300 K. The gas undergoes an isovolumetric process, acquiring 500 J of energy by heat. It then undergoes an isobaric process, losing this same amount of energy by heat. Determine the new temperature of the gas:
questions.explanation:
For monoatomic gas C_V = 1.5R, C_P = 2.5R
At const volume, 
Q = 500 J
Q = nC_V$\Delta$T
500 = 1 $\times$ 1.5 $\times$ 8.31(T_1 - 300)
T_1 = 340K
At const pressure Q = 500 J
Q = nC_P$\Delta$T
500 = 1 $\times$ 1.5 $\times$ 8.31(340 - T_2)
T_2 = 316K

questions.question: One mole of an ideal monatomic gas is at an initial temperature of 300 K. The gas undergoes an isovolumetric process, acquiring 500 J of energy by heat. It then undergoes an isobaric process, losing this same amount of energy by heat. Determine the work done on the gas.
questions.question_raw: One mole of an ideal monatomic gas is at an initial temperature of 300 K. The gas undergoes an isovolumetric process, acquiring 500 J of energy by heat. It then undergoes an isobaric process, losing this same amount of energy by heat. Determine the work done on the gas.
questions.explanation:
At constant volume, 
Q = 500 J
Q = $n{C_P}\Delta T$
500 = 1 $\times$ 2.5 $\times$ 8.31$\Delta T$
$\Delta T$ = 24.06
work done on gas is W = nR$\Delta T$ = 1 $\times$ 8.31 $\times$ 24.06 = 200 J

questions.question: 1 mole of a monoatomic gas is mixed with 3 moles of a diatomic gas. What is the molecular specific heat of the mixture at constant volume?
questions.question_raw: 1 mole of a monoatomic gas is mixed with 3 moles of a diatomic gas. What is the molecular specific heat of the mixture at constant volume?
questions.explanation:
for monoatomic gas 
${C_{V_1}} = {3 \over 2}R$
 for diatomic gas
${C_{V_2}} = {5 \over 2}R $ 
from conservation of energy we get,
C_V = $ {{{n_1}{C_{{V_1}}} + {n_2}{C_{{V_2}}}} \over {{n_1} + {n_2}}} = {{\left( {1 \times 1.5R} \right) + \left( {3 \times 2.5R} \right)} \over {1 + 3}} = {9 \over 4}R$
${C_V} = {9 \over 4} \times 8.31 $ = 18.7J/mol K

questions.question: Two moles of an ideal gas ($\gamma = 1.4$) expands slowly and adiabatically from a pressure of 5.00 atm and a volume of 12.0 L to a final volume of 30.0 L. What is the final pressure of the gas?
questions.question_raw: Two moles of an ideal gas ({tex}\gamma = 1.4{/tex}) expands slowly and adiabatically from a pressure of 5.00 atm and a volume of 12.0 L to a final volume of 30.0 L. What is the final pressure of the gas?

questions.question: Two moles of an ideal gas ($\gamma $ = 1.4) expands slowly and adiabatically from the pressure of 5.00 atm and a volume of 12.0 L to a final volume of 30.0 L. What are the initial and final temperatures?
questions.question_raw: Two moles of an ideal gas ({tex}\gamma {/tex} = 1.4) expands slowly and adiabatically from the pressure of 5.00 atm and a volume of 12.0 L to a final volume of 30.0 L. What are the initial and final temperatures?
questions.explanation:
${T_i} = {{{P_i}{V_i}} \over {nR}} = {{\left( {5 \times 1.01 \times {{10}^5}} \right) \times \left( {12 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}} \right)} \over {2 \times 8.31}} $ = 366K
${P_i}{V_i}^\gamma = {P_f}{V_f}^\gamma $
${P_f} = {P_i}{\left( {{{{V_i}} \over {{V_f}}}} \right)^\gamma } = 5 \times {\left( {{{12} \over {30}}} \right)^{1.4}} $ = 5 $\times$ (0.4)^1.4 = 1.39atm
${T_f} = {{{P_f}{V_f}} \over {nR}} = {{\left( {1.39 \times 1.01 \times {{10}^5}} \right) \times \left( {30 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}} \right)} \over {2 \times 8.31}} $ = 253K

questions.question: Two moles of an ideal gas ($\gamma = 1.4$) expands slowly and adiabatically from the pressure of 5.00 atm and a volume of 12.0 L to a final volume of 30.0 L. Find Q, W, and $\Delta {{\rm{E}}_{{\rm{int}}}}$.
questions.question_raw: Two moles of an ideal gas ({tex}\gamma = 1.4{/tex}) expands slowly and adiabatically from the pressure of 5.00 atm and a volume of 12.0 L to a final volume of 30.0 L. Find Q, W, and {tex}\Delta {{\rm{E}}_{{\rm{int}}}}{/tex}.
questions.explanation:
Process is adiabatic. so that Q = 0
${T_i} = {{{P_i}{V_i}} \over {nR}} = {{\left( {5 \times 1.01 \times {{10}^5}} \right) \times \left( {12 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}} \right)} \over {2 \times 8.31}} $ = 366K
$P_{1} V_{1}^{\gamma}=P_{2} V_{2}^{\gamma}$
$P_{2}=P_{1}\left(\frac{V_{1}}{V_{2}}\right)^{\gamma}=5 \times\left(\frac{12}{30}\right)^{1.4}$ = 5 $\times$ (0.4)^1.4 = 1.39atm
${T_f} = {{{P_f}{V_f}} \over {nR}} = {{\left( {1.39 \times 1.01 \times {{10}^5}} \right) \times \left( {30 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}} \right)} \over {2 \times 8.31}} $ = 253K
For the adiabatic process ; q=0 J
$\Delta U = n{C_V}\Delta T $ 
$\Delta U$= 2 $\times$ 2.5R(253 − 366) = 2 $\times$ 2.5 $\times$ 8.31 $\times$ 13 = −4.66KJ
From first law of thermodynamics ; Q = $\Delta$U + W
0 = −4.66 + W
W = 4.66KJ

questions.question: The air in a thundercloud expands as it rises. If its initial temperature was 300 K, and if no energy is lost by thermal conduction on expansion, what is its temperature when the initial volume has doubled?${\gamma _{air}} $ = 1.41
questions.question_raw: The air in a thundercloud expands as it rises. If its initial temperature was 300 K, and if no energy is lost by thermal conduction on expansion, what is its temperature when the initial volume has doubled?{tex}{\gamma _{air}} {/tex} = 1.41

questions.question: Four liters of a diatomic ideal gas ($\gamma = 1.4$) confined to a cylinder is subject to a closed cycle. Initially, the gas is at 1.00 atm and at 300 K. First, its pressure is tripled under constant volume. Then, it expands adiabatically to its original pressure. Finally, the gas is compressed isobarically to its original volume. Determine the volume of the gas at the end of the adiabatic expansion.
questions.question_raw: Four liters of a diatomic ideal gas ({tex}\gamma = 1.4{/tex}) confined to a cylinder is subject to a closed cycle. Initially, the gas is at 1.00 atm and at 300 K. First, its pressure is tripled under constant volume. Then, it expands adiabatically to its original pressure. Finally, the gas is compressed isobarically to its original volume. Determine the volume of the gas at the end of the adiabatic expansion.
questions.explanation:
$P_{1} V_{1}^{\gamma}=P_{2} V_{2}^{\gamma}$
$V_{2}^{\gamma}=\frac{P_{1}}{P_{2}} V_{1}^{\gamma}$
$V_{2}=\left(\frac{P_{1}}{P_{2}}\right)^{1 \over \gamma} V_{1}=\left(\frac{3}{1}\right)^{1 \over 1.4} \times 4$ = 8.79 lit

questions.question: Four liters of a diatomic ideal gas ($\gamma = 1.4$) confined to a cylinder is subject to a closed cycle. Initially, the gas is at 1.00 atm and at 300 K. First, its pressure is tripled under constant volume. Then, it expands adiabatically to its original pressure. Finally, the gas is compressed isobarically to its original volume. Find the temperature of the gas at the start of the adiabatic expansion.
questions.question_raw: Four liters of a diatomic ideal gas ({tex}\gamma = 1.4{/tex}) confined to a cylinder is subject to a closed cycle. Initially, the gas is at 1.00 atm and at 300 K. First, its pressure is tripled under constant volume. Then, it expands adiabatically to its original pressure. Finally, the gas is compressed isobarically to its original volume. Find the temperature of the gas at the start of the adiabatic expansion.

questions.question: Four liters of a diatomic ideal gas ($\gamma = 1.4$) confined to a cylinder is subject to a closed cycle. Initially, the gas is at 1.00 atm and at 300 K. First, its pressure is tripled under constant volume. Then, it expands adiabatically to its original pressure. Finally, the gas is compressed isobarically to its original volume. Find the temperature at the end of the cycle.
questions.question_raw: Four liters of a diatomic ideal gas ({tex}\gamma = 1.4{/tex}) confined to a cylinder is subject to a closed cycle. Initially, the gas is at 1.00 atm and at 300 K. First, its pressure is tripled under constant volume. Then, it expands adiabatically to its original pressure. Finally, the gas is compressed isobarically to its original volume. Find the temperature at the end of the cycle.
questions.explanation:
After Isochoric process
${{{P_1}} \over {{P_2}}} = {{{T_1}} \over {{T_2}}}$
${1 \over 3} = {{300} \over {{T_2}}}$
T_2 = 900K
after one whole cycle: T_1 = 300K

questions.question: Four liters of a diatomic ideal gas ($\gamma $ = 1.4) confined to a cylinder is subject to a closed cycle. Initially, the gas is at 1.00 atm and at 300 K. First, its pressure is tripled under constant volume. Then, it expands adiabatically to its original pressure. Finally, the gas is compressed isobarically to its original volume. What was the network done for this cycle?
questions.question_raw: Four liters of a diatomic ideal gas ({tex}\gamma {/tex} = 1.4) confined to a cylinder is subject to a closed cycle. Initially, the gas is at 1.00 atm and at 300 K. First, its pressure is tripled under constant volume. Then, it expands adiabatically to its original pressure. Finally, the gas is compressed isobarically to its original volume. What was the network done for this cycle?
questions.explanation:
Work done in the isochoric process W_1 = 0
Work done in the adiabatic process
${W_2} = {1 \over {\gamma - 1}}\left( {{P_2}{V_2} - {P_3}{V_3}} \right)$ 
$P_{2} V_{2}^{\gamma}=P_{3} V_{3}^{\gamma}$
$V_{3}^{\gamma}=\frac{P_{2}}{P_{3}} V_{2}^{\gamma}$
V_3 = $\left(\frac{P_{2}}{P_{3}}\right)^{1 \over \gamma} V_{2}=\left(\frac{3}{1}\right)^{1 \over 1.4} \times 4$ = 8.79 litres
${W_2} = {1 \over {\gamma - 1}}\left( {{P_2}{V_2} - {P_3}{V_3}} \right) = {1 \over {1.4 - 1}} $ $\times$ (12 − 8.79) $\times$ 1.01 $\times$ 10^5 $\times$ 10^−3 = 810J
Work done in the isochoric process 
W_3 = P$\Delta$V = 1 $\times$ 1.01 $\times$ 10^5 $\times$ (4 − 8.79) $\times$10^−3 = −483J
Net work done for cycle
W = W_1 + W_2 + W_3 = 0 + 810 − 483 = 327J

questions.question: Consider 2.00 mol of an ideal diatomic gas. Find the total heat capacity at constant volume and at constant pressure, if the molecules rotate but do not vibrate
questions.question_raw: Consider 2.00 mol of an ideal diatomic gas. Find the total heat capacity at constant volume and at constant pressure, if the molecules rotate but do not vibrate
questions.explanation:
for diatomic gas C_V = 2.5 R, C_P = 3.5 R
Heat capacity = mc = nC
at const. volume Heat capacity = nC_V = 2 $\times$ 2.5 $\times$ 1.986 = 9.95cal/K
at const. pressure Heat capacity = nC_P = 2 $\times$ 3.5 $\times$ 1.986 = 13.9cal/K

questions.question: According to Atomic Hypothesis: little particles of atom
questions.question_raw: According to Atomic Hypothesis: little particles of atom

options.label: A
options.text: repel each other when they are at small distance apart, but repel upon being squeezed into one another

options.label: B
options.text: repel each other when they are at small distance apart, but attract upon being squeezed into one another

options.label: C
options.text: repel each other when they are at large distance apart, but attract upon being separated from one another

options.label: D
options.text: attract each other when they are at small distance apart, but repel upon being squeezed into one another

correct_option_texts: attract each other when they are at small distance apart, but repel upon being squeezed into one another
questions.explanation: At room temperature (=300K) the noble gases are all in the gas phase, they are banging around and colliding into one another like little pool balls. At this temperature, when the atoms collide they appear to elastically bounce off of one another, but this bounce is actually a result of atomic repulsion. The atoms are traveling so fast and they approach each other so quickly that their momentum 'squeezes' them together until the atomic repulsion pushes them back apart.

questions.question: The perfect gas equation can be written as:
questions.question_raw: The perfect gas equation can be written as:
questions.explanation:
PV = nRT
PV = nk_BN_AT
R = k_BN_A, N = nN_A
PV = Nk_BT
n = no. of mole
${{\rm{k}}_{\rm{B}}}$= Boltzmann const
N = no. of molecule
N_A = Avogadro no.
